title: The Brain Does Not Receive Pain — It Constructs It

paragraphs: For most of the history of medicine, pain was understood as a signal: the body was damaged, the damage registered at the site, the signal traveled up the spinal cord to the brain, and the pain was the signal's arrival. The nervous system was a telegraph network; pain was the message. This model — called specificity theory — had an elegant simplicity and was substantially wrong. It could not account for why injured soldiers at Anzio reported little pain during battle and severe pain afterward in hospital. It could not account for phantom limb pain — the vivid, sometimes agonizing pain felt in an arm or leg that no longer exists. It could not account for the documented reduction in pain intensity when patients are shown images of their injuries healing, or for the person who walks on a broken ankle for two hours before realising it is broken.

In 1965, Ronald Melzack and Patrick Wall published a paper in Science titled 'Pain Mechanisms: A New Theory.' Gate control theory proposed that pain signals from the body could be modulated by non-pain signals traveling in parallel pathways — that a gate in the spinal cord could be opened or closed by competing inputs, which was why rubbing a bruised area temporarily reduced the pain, why distraction reduced it, why emotional state altered it. The theory displaced the telegraph model with something more like a signal-processing system, one whose outputs depended on inputs from multiple sources including the brain itself.

Melzack went further in his later work. He proposed that the brain contains a neuromatrix — an innate distributed pattern of neural connections that produces the body-self: the felt sense of having a body and knowing where it is. This neuromatrix is what generates phantom limb pain. The matrix continues to produce its map of the body even after the limb has been removed, and the map includes the sensation the limb was experiencing at the time of loss. The pain is entirely real. The limb is not. The pain was never a signal from the body; it was always the brain's output — the neuromatrix commenting on what it believed the body to be.

The clinical evidence for this is now extensive. Chronic pain patients — people whose pain has persisted long after the original tissue damage has healed — show no detectable pathology at the body site that could account for their symptoms. The pain is maintained by the central nervous system's learned prediction, not by ongoing damage. The brain has been trained to produce pain in this region, and continues to train itself. Treatment that works for chronic pain often targets not the body site but the brain's model: the meaning assigned to the sensation, the expectation of suffering, the narrative the patient tells about what the pain means and what it predicts.

The nocebo effect — the harm caused by expectation — is the cleanest demonstration of what this implies. If patients are told that a treatment commonly causes headaches, the incidence of headaches rises among those who received a placebo. If patients are told that a painful procedure will hurt less if they feel in control, it hurts less; the reduction is measurable in neural imaging. The body is responding to a story the brain is telling about what is happening. The distinction between 'real' pain and 'psychological' pain is not a distinction between two different kinds of pain. It is a distinction between different inputs to the same pain-construction mechanism.

What is left after absorbing this is a changed relationship to sensation itself — not the comfortable conclusion that pain is therefore unreal or controllable. It is neither, and pretending otherwise produces its own damage. But the harder recognition: that what you experience when you are in pain is the brain's model of your body in distress, and this model is not a transparent window onto the physical state. It is an interpretation. It was always an interpretation. And interpretations can be wrong.
essay.blockquote: The pain is always real. Whether it accurately reports what is happening in your body is a separate question.

positions: The discovery that pain is centrally constructed should transform how medicine treats it — shifting from the body site as the primary locus of intervention toward the brain's model of the body, its learned predictions, its narrative about what the sensation means. Chronic pain is not 'in the mind' in the dismissive sense; it is in the brain, which has learned something about this body that may or may not still be true.

The constructivist account of pain risks becoming a culturally convenient tool for dismissing suffering: the history of medicine is full of cases — disproportionately involving women and non-white patients — where pain reports were attributed to psychology rather than physiology, and the shift to 'central sensitisation' as a framework can reproduce the same dismissal in more scientific language. The fact that pain is constructed does not mean the construction is freely revisable or that the person experiencing it is doing so by choice.

number.label: Proportion of chronic lower back pain patients who show detectable structural pathology that could account for their symptoms — which means roughly three-quarters of chronic back pain has no explanation in what the imaging shows.

fragment: The arm had been gone for three years. He still reached for things with it — the ghost motion, the involuntary extension toward doorknobs, cups placed on the wrong side of the table. Sometimes, when the weather changed, the hand that wasn't there would clench into a fist he could not open. He had been told the name for it and understood the mechanism. The knowing did not reach the sensation.

word.origin: German compound from 'Welt' (world) and 'Schmerz' (pain, ache); coined by the German Romantic writer Jean Paul in his 1827 novel 'Selina'; denotes the grief or melancholy arising from comparing the actual state of the world with what one believes it should or could be — a pain not of the body and not caused by any specific injury.
word.definition: World-pain; the sorrow produced by the gap between what exists and what should exist — not located in the body, not caused by any identifiable event, but arising from the structural awareness that reality does not meet the mind's requirements of it. Distinguished from ordinary sadness by its cause: it is the pain of having ideals, the ache of the distance between the world as it is and the world the mind insists on.

quote.text: Whatever pain achieves, it achieves in part through its unsharability, and it ensures this unsharability through its resistance to language.
quote.author: Elaine Scarry

on_this_day.text: On 24 May 1819, the future Queen Victoria was born at Kensington Palace. She would later use chloroform during the births of Prince Leopold (1853) and Princess Beatrice (1857), despite significant medical and religious opposition — the argument against its use being that pain in childbirth was morally necessary, theologically required, part of what birth was meant to be. Her public use of anaesthesia settled the cultural debate by changing the frame: once it was known the Queen had used it, the association between suffering and virtue in this context became difficult to maintain publicly. That this was a framing imposed on pain rather than a property intrinsic to pain was not the lesson that was drawn at the time.

person.name: Ronald Melzack
paragraphs: Ronald Melzack was the Canadian psychologist who, with British physiologist Patrick Wall, published 'Pain Mechanisms: A New Theory' in Science in 1965 — one of the most influential papers in the history of medicine. Gate control theory proposed that pain was not a simple signal from body to brain but a modulated process in which non-pain inputs, emotional state, and top-down signals from the brain could open or close a gate in the spinal cord, altering whether and how strongly pain reached conscious awareness. The theory explained phenomena that specificity theory could not: why rubbing a bruise helps, why distraction reduces pain, why the same injury hurts more at rest than during action. It transformed pain medicine from a field concerned primarily with severing pain pathways into one concerned with the full neural context in which pain was generated.

Melzack's later concept of the neuromatrix — the brain's innate body-map that continues to generate the felt sense of having a body regardless of whether the body parts remain — emerged from his decades of work on phantom limb pain. The matrix concept implied that pain was not merely modulated but produced by the brain: a central output rather than a peripheral input. He spent five decades at McGill University, developing the McGill Pain Questionnaire, now the most widely used pain assessment tool in the world. He died in 2019 at 90, having watched the field he helped found become central to the aftermath of the opioid crisis — a painful irony for the man who had spent his life arguing that the true source of pain was never where medicine had been looking.

now_here.name: Batu Caves
now_here.text: During Thaipusam — held in January or February — devotees at Batu Caves walk for kilometers with steel rods pierced through cheeks and tongue, carrying kavadi: large metal frames mounted to the body through hooks in the skin. Participants report little or no significant pain. The same interventions in a non-ritual context would be experienced as intensely painful. The place is a living demonstration, repeated annually, of the brain's pain-construction mechanism responding to context, meaning, and expectation rather than to tissue damage alone. The limestone caves behind the temple are 400 million years old. The demonstration inside them is as old as the nervous system.

questions.text: If pain is constructed by the brain rather than simply received from the body, does this change how you relate to pain you are currently experiencing — or is the construction invisible from inside the experience?
questions.deeper: Is there a meaningful difference between 'real pain' and pain that persists after its physical cause has resolved? And if there is not, what does that imply about the medical and moral weight we give to verifiable injury as the condition for pain being taken seriously?
questions.experiment: The next time you experience mild pain — a headache, a sore muscle — spend five minutes attending to it with curiosity rather than resistance. Notice whether the quality of the experience changes when the relationship to it changes.

questions.text: The nocebo effect shows that being told something will hurt increases the probability that it does. Where in your own life do you observe a version of this — expectation creating or intensifying the experience it predicts?
questions.deeper: If expectation shapes experience in this way, what follows for the stories you tell yourself about what is likely to be hard? Is managing the narrative the same as managing the reality, or is that distinction exactly the one the constructivist account is trying to dissolve?
questions.experiment: Before an activity you typically find unpleasant, try describing it to yourself in purely neutral physical terms — no anticipatory judgment, only description of what will happen. Notice whether this changes the experience, and if so, whether that change feels like cheating.

questions.text: Elaine Scarry argues that pain is fundamentally private and resistant to language — that to have pain is to have certainty while to hear about it is to have doubt. Does this match your experience of both sides of that gap?
questions.deeper: What does the unsharability of pain imply for the ethics of responding to others' suffering? If you cannot fully know what another person's pain is, what remains as the basis for care?
questions.experiment: Try to describe a pain you have experienced to someone who has not had it — not its location and intensity but what it was actually like from inside it. Notice where the description breaks down and what breaks down first.

trivia.question: Gate control theory, proposed by Melzack and Wall in 1965, showed that pain signals could be modulated in the spinal cord. What type of nerve fibers carry the competing signals that can 'close the gate' and reduce pain?
options: C fibers — slow, unmyelinated fibers that carry dull, aching, and burning pain
A-delta fibers — fast-conducting fibers that carry sharp, acute, well-localised pain
A-beta fibers — large, fast-conducting fibers that carry touch, pressure, and vibration
Sympathetic efferent fibers — part of the autonomic nervous system controlling involuntary responses
trivia.explanation: A-beta fibers are large-diameter, well-myelinated, fast-conducting nerve fibers that normally carry touch, pressure, and vibration signals. In gate control theory, activating A-beta fibers — through rubbing, pressure, or vibration near a pain site — inhibits the spinal interneurons that would otherwise transmit pain signals upward. This is the mechanism behind why rubbing a bruised area temporarily reduces pain. The gate can also be influenced by descending signals from the brain — which is why emotional state, attention, and expectation can independently alter pain intensity regardless of what is happening in the tissue.

clues: Its name comes from the Latin for 'I will harm'
A completely inert substance can produce genuine, measurable physical symptoms through this mechanism
In clinical trials, participants warned of a side effect experience it at higher rates than those given identical treatment without the warning
It is the reason that informed consent in medicine has to balance transparency against the risk of producing what it describes

movie.title: The Diving Bell and the Butterfly
movie.text: Jean-Dominique Bauby, editor of French Elle, suffers a catastrophic stroke and wakes into locked-in syndrome: his mind intact, his body paralysed except for one blinking eye. The film renders his inner life with hallucinatory vividness while showing the body that can no longer express or enact it. It belongs near this essay not because of what it says about pain specifically but because of what it shows about the gap between the brain's reality — vivid, mobile, still feeling — and the body's state. Bauby dictated his memoir by blinking through the alphabet; it was published two days before his death.
